posts.title: Kong Concentrates: The Story Behind Our Brand
posts.description: Two people, one lab, Grove Oklahoma. The story behind Kong Concentrates — our founding, philosophy, competition history, and why quality outranks everything.
posts.og_title: Kong Concentrates: The Story Behind Our Brand
posts.body:
There's a reason the gorilla is thinking.

He isn't roaring. He isn't beating his chest. He's sitting the way Rodin's Thinker sits — hunched forward, hand pressed to his chin, locked in deep concentration. That image isn't random. It's the entire philosophy of Kong Concentrates compressed into a single frame.

We are two people in Grove, Oklahoma who decided that if quality solventless hash didn't exist at the level we expected, we'd build the operation to produce it ourselves. That decision has driven every move we've made since 2019.

## Where It Started

Kong Concentrates was founded in 2019 with one focus: solventless hash products made the right way. By 2020 we had our lab open and production underway — live hash rosin, traditional aged hash, full-melt, rosin vapes, and hash holes. No solvents, no shortcuts, no compromise on starting material.

That last part became a problem.

Oklahoma's cannabis market was growing fast, but access to truly elite fresh-frozen material — the kind that produces six-star full-melt — was inconsistent at best. You can have the best pressing technique in the world, but if the flower isn't there, the rosin won't be either. We weren't willing to put out product we didn't believe in, so we made the decision most small operators don't: we built our own cultivation facility.

Construction started at the end of 2021. By 2022 we had opened the doors and begun single-source production — growing our own cultivars, fresh-freezing at harvest, washing our own hash, and pressing our own rosin. Everything under one roof, controlled start to finish by the same two people who started this brand.

## The Team

Kong Concentrates is Garrett and Chanston. Owner and Partner/Director of Operations respectively, with over 30 years of combined industry experience between the two of us. No investors. No outside operators. Entirely self-funded and self-run.

That's not something we say as a badge of suffering — it's a deliberate choice. When you answer to no one but the product, the product gets better. Every decision we make goes directly into what ends up in the jar.

## Competition and Recognition

We've never chased volume. We've chased standard — and that's led us onto some of the most respected stages in the solventless world.

We've been featured in Frenchy Cannoli's Hash Porn Competition for our traditional hash photography — a nod to one of the most legendary figures in global hash culture. We've competed at Legends of Hashish, Hash Madness, Squish Off, and the Cannifest MoLuv Headstash Bowl, where we took home 3rd place. These events aren't marketing opportunities. They're calibration — a chance to measure our work against the best operators in the country and come back sharper.

Traditional aged hash, in particular, is something we take seriously in ways most domestic producers don't. It's a craft that requires time, patience, and an understanding of what the plant is doing at every stage. The fact that our photos were recognized in Frenchy's competition — named for the man who spent decades documenting and preserving traditional hashish culture — means something to us.

## The Kong Philosophy

The gorilla's name is Kong. And as the name suggests, most people assume Kong is king.

He isn't.

That's the whole point. Our slogan — Quality is King — is a deliberate inversion. Kong is powerful, Kong is focused, Kong is constantly working — but quality outranks him. Quality is the thing he answers to. The thing we all answer to.

The Thinker reference runs even deeper than the visual. Rodin's sculpture was created to represent the creative mind in struggle — intellect at work, wrestling with craft and meaning. A gorilla in that same posture, concentrating over the product in front of him, is us. That's what every day in the lab looks like. Deliberate, quiet, focused effort toward something better than what we made last time.

The "jungle" metaphor isn't lost on us either. Anyone who's spent time in a cannabis grow room knows it — the heat, the humidity, the dense canopy of plants pressing in from every direction. We built our operation in that jungle, and we're at home in it.

## What We Make

Our current lineup covers the full range of premium solventless:

- **Live Hash Rosin** — fresh-frozen flower, ice water extracted, freeze-dried, and pressed at low temperature to preserve the full terpene profile
- **Traditional Aged Hash** — old-world technique with modern sourcing standards
- **Full-Melt Hash** — the benchmark of solventless quality, rated by how completely it vaporizes
- **Rosin Vapes** — solventless hardware, no distillate, no cutting agents
- **Hash Holes** — premium flower rolls with a live rosin core, engineered to burn from the inside out

We're expanding. New product formats, new cultivars, statewide growth across Oklahoma. The operation that started as two people and a pressing vision is becoming something larger — without losing the thing that made it worth building in the first place.

## Why It Matters

The solventless market is full of brands that use the right words. Craft. Artisan. Small batch. Pure. Most of them are positioned around an aesthetic rather than a process.

We are the process. We grow the flower, wash the hash, press the rosin, and put our name on it. When you pick up a Kong product, you're holding something that two people made from the ground up — from seed to jar — in a facility they built with their own hands in Grove, Oklahoma.

The gorilla is still thinking. He's got more to concentrate on.

*Quality is King.*

---

*Kong Concentrates is a solventless hash brand based in Grove, Oklahoma. Founded in 2019, we produce live hash rosin, full-melt hash, traditional aged hash, rosin vapes, and hash holes — all single-source, all solventless, all tested by third-party labs. Find us at licensed dispensaries across Oklahoma.*

posts.title: What Is Live Hash Rosin? A Complete Guide to Solventless Concentrates
posts.description: Live hash rosin is the pinnacle of solventless cannabis extraction. Learn exactly what it is, how it's made, and why the starting material makes all the difference.
posts.og_title: What Is Live Hash Rosin? A Complete Guide
posts.body:
If you've spent any time in a premium dispensary lately, you've seen the term "live hash rosin" on labels — usually attached to a jar with a price tag that makes you look twice. Most budtenders will tell you it's "the cleanest" or "the top shelf" option. That's true, but it doesn't explain *why*.

Live hash rosin is what happens when you refuse to take any shortcuts. Here's the full breakdown.

## What "Live" Actually Means

The word "live" in cannabis concentrates refers to **fresh-frozen starting material**. Instead of drying and curing harvested flower the traditional way, live processing begins the moment the plant is cut: the fresh biomass goes directly into a deep freeze — typically around -20°F — to lock in the cannabinoid and terpene profile at its absolute peak.

Why does that matter? Drying and curing cannabis is a necessary step for smokable flower, but it's destructive to terpenes. Heat, oxygen, and time degrade the volatile aromatic compounds responsible for the taste, smell, and much of the effect of cannabis. Fresh-freezing bypasses that degradation entirely. The plant that goes into the freezer is as close as you'll get to the living plant in the field.

The alternative — making concentrate from dried, cured flower — is called "cured rosin." It's still solventless, but it starts with a less complete terpene profile. Live hash rosin preserves more of the original plant chemistry.

## The Two-Stage Extraction Process

Live hash rosin is made in two distinct phases: ice water extraction and pressing.

**Phase 1 — Ice Water Hash (Bubble Hash)**

Fresh-frozen flower is submerged in ice water and agitated. The cold temperature and mechanical motion cause the trichome heads — the resin-producing structures on cannabis — to become brittle and break free from the plant material. The water is then filtered through a series of mesh bags at progressively finer micron sizes, separating trichomes by size and quality.

The collected material is freeze-dried to remove all moisture, producing what's known as **bubble hash** or **ice water hash**. The quality grade — from one to six stars — reflects how completely and cleanly the hash vaporizes. Six-star full-melt hash is the benchmark: it bubbles, melts, and vanishes cleanly on a nail, leaving no residue.

**Phase 2 — Pressing**

The freeze-dried hash is then pressed between heated plates — a rosin press — at carefully controlled temperature and pressure. This mechanical pressure squeezes the resinous oil out of the trichome material without adding any solvents or chemicals. What flows out is live hash rosin: a full-spectrum extract that contains the cannabinoids, terpenes, and other plant compounds present in the original fresh-frozen material.

Temperature control during pressing is critical. Too hot and you volatilize terpenes; too cold and the rosin won't flow properly. Experienced processors develop a feel for the right combination based on the specific cultivar and batch.

## Why Starting Material Determines Everything

There's a phrase in the solventless world: **"you can't polish a turd."** No amount of technical skill in the wash room or at the press compensates for mediocre starting material.

This is why single-source operations — where the producer grows their own flower, fresh-freezes it at harvest, and processes it themselves — produce the most consistent results. When you grow the plant, you control the genetics, the environment, the harvest timing, and the freeze. Every variable that affects the final product is in your hands.

At Kong Concentrates, we made the decision to build our own cultivation facility specifically because we couldn't source fresh-frozen material at the standard we required. Our cultivation opened in 2022, and since then every Kong product begins with flower grown, harvested, and frozen in our own facility in Grove, Oklahoma. The cultivars in our lineup — Bacio Mints, Devil Driver, Grape Pie, Sticky Buns, and others — are selected for their trichome density, terpene expression, and how they respond to ice water extraction.

## What to Look for When You Buy

Not all live hash rosin is the same. Here's what separates a premium product from a mediocre one:

- **Starting material quality** — Was it grown for hash production, or is it a trim run? Fresh-frozen whole flower produces better results than trim.
- **Wash quality** — The hash used for pressing should be high-grade (5- or 6-star). Lower-quality hash pressed into rosin still produces lower-quality rosin.
- **Terpene preservation** — Fresh, flavorful rosin indicates good handling. Bland or "cooked" flavor suggests improper processing temperatures.
- **Third-party COA** — Any reputable producer should have a current Certificate of Analysis showing cannabinoid and residual solvent testing. (Spoiler: in solventless, there are no residual solvents.)
- **Texture** — Live hash rosin typically presents as a badder, jam, or diamonds-and-sauce consistency depending on the cultivar and post-processing. Neither is inherently better; consistency varies by plant.

## The Honest Case for Paying More

Live hash rosin is labor-intensive. The fresh-freeze, the multiple-bag wash, the freeze-dry, the press, the post-processing — each step requires equipment, time, and skill. The yield from fresh-frozen flower is lower than solvent-based extractions. You're paying for a process that refuses shortcuts.

For consumers who care about what they're inhaling — no residual solvents, no cutting agents, no artificial terpenes — live hash rosin is the straightforward answer. It's the plant, extracted mechanically, nothing added.

That's the whole product. That's the whole point.

---

*Kong Concentrates produces single-source live hash rosin in Grove, Oklahoma. All products are third-party lab tested. Find us at licensed dispensaries across Oklahoma.*

posts.title: What Is Full-Melt Hash? Understanding the Solventless Gold Standard
posts.description: Full-melt hash is the highest quality grade of ice water hash — the benchmark that serious solventless producers chase. Here's what it is, how it's graded, and why it's so hard to make.
posts.og_title: What Is Full-Melt Hash? The Solventless Gold Standard Explained
posts.body:
The phrase "full-melt" gets used a lot in solventless cannabis circles. Sometimes it's used correctly. More often it isn't. It's worth understanding what the term actually means — because once you know, you'll understand why truly great hash is so rare and so expensive.

## The Hash Star Rating System

Ice water hash — also called bubble hash — is graded on a scale from one to six stars. The rating reflects one specific quality: how cleanly the hash vaporizes on a heated surface.

- **1–2 stars** — Low-quality hash with significant plant contamination. Burns black, leaves substantial residue. Used for edibles or pressing into rosin rather than direct consumption.
- **3–4 stars** — Half-melt territory. Will vaporize partially but leaves noticeable charred residue. Still usable; often pressed into rosin.
- **5 stars** — High-quality hash that melts cleanly with minor residue. Acceptable for direct dabbing or pressing.
- **6 stars** — Full melt. When placed on a heated nail or banger, it bubbles up, vaporizes completely, and leaves virtually no residue. This is the benchmark.

The star rating isn't arbitrary. It's a direct measure of **trichome head purity** — how much of the material is actually resinous trichome heads versus plant material, trichome stalks, and other contamination. Higher purity means cleaner melt.

## Why Full-Melt Is So Difficult to Produce

Producing six-star full-melt consistently requires everything to go right at once. There are no shortcuts and no hiding places.

**Genetics matter most.** Not all cannabis strains produce trichomes that wash cleanly into full-melt material. Some cultivars produce abundant resin that clings to plant material and doesn't separate cleanly. Others produce smaller trichome heads that fall through the wrong mesh screens. Finding cultivars that produce clean, large-headed trichomes that wash into high-grade material is a process of trial and error that takes years.

**Harvest timing is critical.** Trichomes reach peak head development at a specific point in the flower's maturity. Harvest too early and the heads are underdeveloped; too late and they begin to degrade. For fresh-frozen material destined for full-melt production, the window between "perfect" and "past it" can be a matter of days.

**The freeze must be immediate and maintained.** From the moment the plant is cut, degradation begins. The faster the biomass reaches freezing temperature — and the more consistent that temperature is maintained through storage and transport — the better the hash will be. Any temperature fluctuation causes trichome damage.

**Wash conditions require precision.** Water temperature, agitation speed, agitation duration, and the specific combination of micron screens all affect hash quality. Cold water keeps trichomes brittle and helps them separate cleanly. Aggressive agitation can break stalks along with heads, introducing contamination. Experienced hash washers develop a feel for each cultivar — how long it needs, how gently it should be handled.

**Freeze-drying instead of air-drying.** Wet hash dried in open air (even with fans and climate control) risks mold and loses terpenes to evaporation. Freeze-drying — lyophilization — removes moisture through sublimation under vacuum, preserving structure and terpenes. It's the difference between proper preservation and improvisation.

## Full-Melt as a Pressing Material

Full-melt hash is exceptional consumed directly — on a nail, in a hash bowl, or in traditional pressed hash form. But it's also the best possible starting material for live hash rosin.

When you press five- or six-star hash under controlled heat and pressure, the resulting rosin carries the full terpene richness of the hash. Lower-grade starting material produces rosin with more plant contamination and a less complete aromatic profile. The best live hash rosin begins with the best hash.

At Kong Concentrates, the wash quality of our starting material directly determines which products it becomes. Our highest-grade wash material goes toward direct full-melt offerings and premium rosin. We don't blend quality levels or mix batches — each run is assessed independently based on what came out of the wash.

## How to Consume Full-Melt Hash

Full-melt hash is best consumed at lower temperatures than typical BHO concentrates. Because it's a whole-spectrum product — containing the full range of terpenes, minor cannabinoids, and plant compounds — it's more sensitive to heat.

Low-temp dabbing (around 400–450°F) preserves terpene expression and produces a smooth, flavorful experience. High-temp dabs vaporize everything quickly but sacrifice much of the aromatic complexity that makes full-melt worth buying in the first place.

Hash bowls — mixing full-melt into flower in a pipe or bong — are the traditional method and remain an excellent option. The hash melts into the flower as it burns, providing a more gradual, layered effect.

## The Bottom Line

Full-melt hash is the hardest thing in the solventless world to produce well. It requires elite genetics, perfect harvest timing, controlled fresh-freezing, precise washing, proper freeze-drying, and careful handling all the way through to the jar. If any single step fails, the quality drops.

When you see true six-star full-melt on a dispensary shelf — with a COA confirming its quality — you're looking at the result of a producer who got every step right. That's not common. When it happens, it matters.

---

*Kong Concentrates produces full-melt ice water hash and live hash rosin in Grove, Oklahoma. All products are single-source and third-party lab tested. Available at licensed dispensaries across Oklahoma.*

posts.title: Hash Holes Explained: What They Are and Why They Burn the Way They Do
posts.description: Hash holes are premium infused pre-rolls with a live rosin core that burns from the inside out. Here's exactly how they're made and what makes a great one.
posts.og_title: Hash Holes Explained: What They Are and Why They Burn the Way They Do
posts.body:
Hash holes look simple: a pre-roll with a rosin core running through the center. What actually happens when you light one — and why the best ones behave differently from cheap imitations — is worth understanding before you buy.

## What a Hash Hole Actually Is

A hash hole is a cannabis pre-roll constructed with a rope of live hash rosin running lengthwise through the interior of the roll. When the joint burns, the outer flower combusts normally while the rosin core vaporizes from the inside. The result is a gap — the "hole" — that forms in the cherry as the concentrate burns out faster than the surrounding flower.

The concept became widely popularized around 2020–2021, but the fundamental idea — putting hash inside a joint — is ancient. The modern version is distinguished by the quality of the rosin used and the precision of the construction.

## Construction: Why It's Harder Than It Looks

Making a hash hole that burns correctly requires solving a physics problem.

Live rosin and cannabis flower have different combustion properties. Rosin is much denser than flower and requires more heat to vaporize. If the rosin core is too thick, it won't keep pace with the burning flower and will choke the cherry. If it's too thin, you lose the effect. The ratio between flower density and rosin core diameter has to be dialed in for each cultivar combination.

The rosin also needs to be at the right consistency for rolling. Too cold and it's brittle and breaks when you try to form a rope. Too warm and it's sticky and deforms. Experienced hash hole makers know the working temperature for each product — and the window is narrower than it sounds.

The paper and filter selection matter too. Hash holes burn hotter than regular joints, and thin paper burns unevenly around the denser core. Proper construction requires a paper that can handle the heat gradient between the burning flower and the vaporizing rosin.

## The Burn: What to Expect

A properly made hash hole burns in a specific way. As you smoke, the cherry develops a visible gap or "hole" in the center where the rosin core has vaporized ahead of the surrounding flower. The flower forms a "canoeing" ring around this hole as it burns.

The hole is actually an indicator of quality. It means the rosin is vaporizing completely — not getting clogged, not going unburned, not simply charring. If the hole doesn't form, the rosin isn't melting properly, either because the construction is off or the rosin quality is too low.

A hash hole that burns well delivers rosin vapor and flower smoke simultaneously. The flavor is more complex than either alone — the terpene-rich rosin running through the more muted background of combusted flower. Experienced consumers learn to take slower, lower-temperature draws to get the most out of the rosin component.

## Rosin Quality Is the Core Differentiator

The phrase "hash hole" can technically describe any pre-roll with any rosin inside it. The difference between a premium hash hole and a budget one is entirely the quality of the rosin core.

Low-quality or improperly stored rosin can go rancid, produce harsh smoke, or fail to vaporize cleanly. Distillate-infused "hash holes" — which aren't hash holes at all — replace solventless rosin with solvent-extracted distillate, producing a fundamentally different product despite the similar label.

At Kong Concentrates, our hash holes use live hash rosin pressed from our own single-source flower. The rosin core is made the same way as our standalone rosin products — from fresh-frozen flower, washed into ice water hash, and pressed at controlled temperatures. The hash hole format is a different delivery mechanism for the same quality material.

Our current hash hole offerings include cultivar-specific and cross-cultivar combinations — Grape Pie x Devil Driver and Creme Soda + Pink Runtz, among others — where the rosin core and the surrounding flower are selected to complement each other's terpene profiles.

## Are Hash Holes Worth It?

Hash holes cost more than regular pre-rolls. The math is straightforward: you're paying for the gram (or more) of live rosin inside, plus the labor of the construction. A well-made hash hole from a legitimate solventless producer represents the same quality commitment as their standalone rosin, just in a smokable format.

For consumers who prefer smoking over dabbing but want solventless quality, hash holes are the natural answer. The experience is different from a dab — slower, more extended, less intense per hit — but the material is the same.

The hole tells you it's working. Trust the hole.

---

*Kong Concentrates produces live rosin hash holes in Grove, Oklahoma using single-source fresh-frozen flower. Find our current cultivar lineup at licensed dispensaries across Oklahoma.*

posts.title: Solventless vs. Solvent-Based Concentrates: What's Actually Different
posts.description: Rosin vs. BHO, solventless vs. solvent-based — what do these terms mean, and does it matter for the consumer? A clear breakdown with no marketing spin.
posts.og_title: Solventless vs. Solvent-Based Concentrates: What's Actually Different
posts.body:
"Solventless" appears on more and more cannabis labels. Like most marketing terms that enter mainstream use, it's starting to lose precision — applied to products that aren't truly solventless, or used as a vague quality signal without explanation.

Here's what solventless actually means, what the alternatives are, and why the distinction matters for some consumers and not at all for others.

## How Cannabis Concentrates Are Made: Two Paths

Every cannabis concentrate is the product of an extraction — a process that separates the desired compounds (cannabinoids, terpenes) from the plant material. The fundamental split is between extractions that use a chemical solvent and those that don't.

**Solvent-based extraction** uses a chemical — typically butane, propane, ethanol, or CO2 — to strip the resin from the plant. The solvent is introduced to the cannabis, dissolves the resinous compounds, and is then removed through evaporation, purging, or distillation. The resulting concentrate is what remains after the solvent is removed.

Common solvent-based products include:
- **BHO (Butane Hash Oil)** — shatter, wax, budder, sauce, live resin
- **PHO (Propane Hash Oil)** — similar to BHO, slightly different terpene profile
- **Ethanol extract** — used in many vape cartridges and tinctures
- **CO2 extract** — supercritical CO2 extraction, common in vape carts

**Solventless extraction** uses only mechanical force, water, heat, and pressure. No chemical solvents are introduced to the plant material at any point.

Common solventless products include:
- **Rosin** — heat and pressure applied to flower or hash
- **Ice water hash / bubble hash** — cold water agitation to separate trichomes
- **Dry sift** — mechanical sifting of dried plant material
- **Kief** — fine trichome material collected through mesh, the simplest form

## The Residual Solvent Question

The most common argument for solventless concentrates is the absence of residual solvents. When a chemical solvent is used in extraction, trace amounts can remain in the final product even after purging. Regulatory testing in legal markets sets limits on acceptable residual solvent levels — in Oklahoma, for example, concentrates are tested against established thresholds for butane, propane, and other solvents.

Reputable solvent-based producers purge their products thoroughly and test for residuals. A compliant BHO or live resin product that passes state testing contains residual solvents at very low levels — below the regulatory threshold for safety.

Solventless concentrates don't have this issue by definition. No solvent is introduced, so there's nothing to purge or test for. The absence of residual solvents in solventless products is structural, not a matter of how well the producer does their job.

Whether trace residual solvents at compliant levels are meaningful to a consumer is a personal decision. The relevant point is that solventless removes the variable entirely.

## Live Resin vs. Live Rosin: A Common Confusion

Both "live resin" and "live rosin" use fresh-frozen starting material, which creates frequent confusion. The difference is the extraction method.

**Live resin** is a solvent-based extract (typically BHO) made from fresh-frozen flower. The fresh-freeze preserves terpenes, and the butane extraction pulls those terpenes along with the cannabinoids. Live resin is often highly terpy and flavorful — but it's a solvent extract.

**Live rosin** is a solventless extract made from fresh-frozen flower via ice water hash and heat press. Same starting material philosophy, entirely different extraction process.

Both can be excellent products. They're not the same category.

## Does Solventless Mean Better?

Not automatically. The quality of any concentrate — solventless or not — depends primarily on:

1. **Starting material** — genetics, cultivation quality, freshness
2. **Extraction skill** — whether the processor knows what they're doing
3. **Post-processing** — storage, temperature management, handling

Poorly washed bubble hash pressed into mediocre rosin by an inexperienced operator is worse than expertly processed live resin from elite genetics. The extraction method doesn't determine quality; the starting material and the producer's skill do.

What solventless *does* guarantee is the absence of chemical solvents in the process. For consumers who want to know with certainty that their concentrate contains no residual butane, propane, or ethanol, solventless is the straightforward answer. It's not a quality guarantee — it's a process guarantee.

## How Kong Concentrates Approaches This

We make only solventless products. That's not a marketing position — it's how we started and the only category we operate in. Our full lineup — live hash rosin, full-melt hash, traditional aged hash, rosin vapes, and hash holes — uses ice water extraction and mechanical pressing exclusively.

We're also single-source: we grow our own flower, fresh-freeze at harvest, and process everything in our own facility in Grove, Oklahoma. The solventless commitment doesn't mean much if the starting material is mediocre, so we control the starting material.

For consumers who are already committed to solventless, we hope the work speaks for itself. For consumers still figuring out what category they want to explore, the most honest advice we can give is this: find a producer who's transparent about their process, tests their products, and shows their work. That standard applies regardless of which extraction method they use.

---

*Kong Concentrates is a solventless concentrate producer based in Grove, Oklahoma. All products are single-source and third-party lab tested. Find us at licensed dispensaries across Oklahoma.*

posts.title: How to Read a Cannabis Certificate of Analysis (COA)
posts.description: A Certificate of Analysis tells you exactly what's in your cannabis product — if you know how to read it. Here's a practical guide to making sense of lab results.
keywords: how to read cannabis lab results
posts.og_title: How to Read a Cannabis Certificate of Analysis (COA)
posts.body:
Every licensed cannabis product in Oklahoma must be tested by a third-party laboratory before it reaches a dispensary shelf. The result of that testing is a Certificate of Analysis — a COA — that lists what's in the product and at what levels.

Most consumers never look at them. That's a missed opportunity. A COA contains the most honest information you'll get about a cannabis product, and reading one is simpler than it looks.

## What a COA Actually Is

A Certificate of Analysis is a lab report issued by an accredited third-party laboratory — a lab that has no financial relationship with the producer — documenting the chemical composition of a specific product batch. In Oklahoma, COAs are required by the Oklahoma Medical Marijuana Authority (OMMA) before a product can be sold legally.

The key phrase is **third-party**. The lab is paid to test the product accurately, not to make it look good. A producer who passes you a COA from a credentialed lab is showing you independent verification of their product's contents.

## The Sections of a COA

**Cannabinoid Profile**

This is usually the first and most prominent section. It lists the measured levels of individual cannabinoids in the product, typically expressed as a percentage (%) for flower and concentrates, or milligrams per gram (mg/g).

The cannabinoids you'll most commonly see:
- **THC (Δ9-THC)** — the primary psychoactive compound
- **THCA** — the acidic precursor to THC, non-psychoactive until heated (decarboxylated)
- **Total THC** — calculated as THCA × 0.877 + THC; this is the activated potency
- **CBD** — the primary non-psychoactive cannabinoid
- **CBG, CBN, CBC** — minor cannabinoids present in smaller amounts

For concentrates, total THC percentages commonly range from 65% to 85%+. Higher isn't always better — a 75% THC rosin with a rich terpene profile will often deliver a more complete experience than a 90% distillate with stripped terpenes added back.

**Terpene Profile**

Not all labs test terpenes, and not all COAs include them. When they do, this section lists the identified aromatic compounds and their concentrations, usually in mg/g or as a percentage.

Terpenes are what drive the flavor and aroma of cannabis. They also interact with cannabinoids in ways that affect the experience — a phenomenon sometimes called the entourage effect. Common terpenes you'll see include myrcene, limonene, caryophyllene, linalool, terpinolene, and ocimene, among many others.

For solventless concentrates like live hash rosin, a robust terpene profile is a quality indicator. Rosin made from fresh-frozen, properly handled starting material should carry the full terpene signature of the original cultivar. If a rosin COA shows minimal terpene content, that's worth noting.

**Residual Solvents**

This section is relevant for solvent-based concentrates. It lists tested solvents — butane, propane, ethanol, etc. — and their measured levels in parts per million (ppm). Oklahoma sets maximum allowable levels for each solvent; a passing result means all residuals are below those thresholds.

For **solventless products** — rosin, ice water hash, dry sift — this section should either be absent (no solvents used, no residuals to detect) or show non-detected (ND) results across the board. If you see a solventless product with detected solvent residuals, that's a red flag.

**Microbiological Testing**

This section tests for harmful microorganisms — specifically things like E. coli, Salmonella, and certain molds (Aspergillus species). All must test below action limits to pass. A product with failed microbial testing cannot legally be sold.

**Heavy Metals**

Cannabis is a bioaccumulator — it absorbs compounds from its growing medium including heavy metals. Lead, cadmium, arsenic, and mercury are tested against established limits. This is especially relevant for products derived from outdoor or mixed-light cultivation, where soil composition varies more than in controlled indoor environments.

**Pesticide Screening**

Residual pesticides from cultivation are tested against a list of regulated compounds. Any detected pesticide above its action limit results in a failed test.

## How to Verify a COA Is Real

A COA is only useful if it's authentic. Legitimate COAs include:
- The name and license number of the testing laboratory
- The date of testing
- The batch or sample ID matching the product label
- A signature or certification from the lab director

In Oklahoma, you can cross-reference a lab's license through the OMMA. Some labs also publish COAs to a searchable database linked through QR codes on product labels — scan the code and the COA should appear directly from the lab's system, not just a PDF the producer uploaded.

At Kong Concentrates, our COAs are issued by licensed third-party Oklahoma labs and are available for all current products. Our solventless lineup shows what you'd expect: no residual solvents, full cannabinoid profiles, and terpene testing on applicable products.

## The Bottom Line

A COA won't tell you whether you'll like a product. Taste is subjective and potency interacts differently with different people. But a COA will tell you exactly what's in the jar — and more importantly, what isn't.

For any concentrate purchase, it's worth asking for the COA if it isn't displayed. A producer who makes it difficult to access current lab results is a producer who doesn't want you looking too closely.

Look closely.

---

*Kong Concentrates lab results are available for all current products. We're based in Grove, Oklahoma and available at licensed dispensaries across the state.*

posts.title: Traditional Aged Hash: Ancient Craft, Modern Standards
posts.description: Traditional aged hash is one of the oldest cannabis preparations in the world. Here's what it is, how it's made, and why so few domestic producers do it correctly.
posts.og_title: Traditional Aged Hash: Ancient Craft, Modern Standards
posts.body:
Most cannabis concentrates are a product of the last twenty years. Traditional aged hash is a product of the last several thousand.

Hashish — compressed and processed cannabis resin — has been produced and traded across Central Asia, the Middle East, and North Africa for centuries. The methods evolved region by region: hand-rubbed charas from the Hindu Kush, dry-sifted pollen hash from Morocco and Lebanon, pressed slabs from Afghanistan. Each tradition developed its own approach to handling the plant, separating the resin, and aging the product into its final form.

The modern domestic cannabis market largely skipped over this history. The solventless revival of the last decade has brought fresh-pressed rosin and ice water hash into the spotlight — but traditional aged hash remains a niche product made by a small number of producers who've taken the time to learn the craft. We're one of them.

## What Traditional Aged Hash Is

Traditional aged hash begins with dry-sift or hand-rubbed resin collection — the same foundational methods used for centuries before ice water extraction existed. The plant material is worked at low temperatures, causing trichome heads to separate from the plant and collect as a fine powder (dry sift) or accumulate on the hands and then be rolled into balls or slabs (hand-rubbed charas).

The collected resin is then worked — kneaded, pressed, heated gently — to break down the trichome cell walls and allow the resinous contents to bind together. This working process is where most of the craft knowledge lives. Pressure, temperature, duration, and technique all affect the final texture, color, and aroma of the hash.

The "aged" part is what separates traditional hash from fresh-pressed product. After initial formation, aged hash is allowed to cure over time — weeks to months in controlled conditions. During aging, enzymatic processes and oxidation transform the aroma and flavor profile. Fresh hash is often grassy and raw-smelling; properly aged hash develops a complex, rounded character that fresh product doesn't have. This transformation is analogous to the maturation of wine or the curing of fine tobacco — time doing work that no process can shortcut.

## The Frenchy Cannoli Connection

No discussion of traditional aged hash in the modern cannabis world is complete without acknowledging Frenchy Cannoli, who spent decades traveling the hash-producing regions of the world — Morocco, Lebanon, Afghanistan, Nepal, India — documenting the traditional methods before they disappeared.

Frenchy dedicated his later years to preserving and teaching these techniques to the Western cannabis industry. His Hash Porn Competition — an event celebrating the photography of traditional hash — became a touchstone for producers attempting to carry this craft forward.

Kong Concentrates has been featured in Frenchy's Hash Porn Competition. That recognition matters to us not as a marketing credential but as confirmation that what we're doing is recognizable to someone who spent a lifetime studying the real thing. Frenchy passed away in 2021, but his documentation and the community he built continue to be the primary resource for producers working in this category.

## Why Most Domestic Producers Don't Make It

Traditional aged hash is time-consuming, low-yielding, and doesn't fit the production model of most cannabis operations.

Dry sift and hand-rubbing collect resin less efficiently than ice water extraction. You process more material for less output. The aging process ties up finished product for weeks or months before it's ready to sell. There's no shortcut — attempting to rush the aging process produces inferior hash.

The craft knowledge is also largely oral and observational. Unlike rosin pressing, where you can purchase a press, set temperatures, and learn from online guides, traditional aged hash involves understanding the resin's behavior through touch and smell across the entire process. It takes failures to develop the feel for it.

At Kong Concentrates, traditional aged hash represents a commitment to the full breadth of solventless craft — not just the modern formats that the domestic market rewards most readily. We produce it because it's worth producing, not because it's efficient.

## How to Consume It

Traditional hash is consumed differently than rosin or bubble hash. It's not typically dabbed. The historic and most natural consumption methods are:

- **Crumbled into flower** in a pipe, bong, or joint — the hash melts as the flower burns
- **Hot-knifed** — two heated metal blades pressed around a piece of hash to vaporize it, an old method still practiced
- **Smoked directly** — some traditional hash is stable enough to be worked into a rope or pellet and smoked on its own
- **Rolled into a hash cigarette** (spliff) — common in European hash culture

The experience is different from concentrated rosin dabs — slower onset, longer duration, and a character that reflects the cultivar and the aging process rather than a pure cannabinoid punch.

## The Standard We Hold

Producing traditional hash that genuinely reflects old-world craft requires starting material that's clean, properly dried, and selected for resin content — and it requires technique developed through practice. We don't approach this product casually.

If you haven't tried traditional aged hash, it's worth finding a quality producer and trying it once. The experience is different enough from modern concentrates that it reads almost like a different product category. In a sense, it is.

---

*Kong Concentrates produces traditional aged hash alongside our full solventless lineup in Grove, Oklahoma. All products are third-party lab tested. Find us at licensed dispensaries across Oklahoma.*

posts.title: Rosin Vapes: What Makes a Solventless Vape Different
posts.description: Most vape cartridges contain distillate and added terpenes. Rosin vapes are different — here's what's actually inside a solventless vape and why it matters.
keywords: what is in a vape cart
posts.og_title: Rosin Vapes: What Makes a Solventless Vape Different
posts.body:
The cannabis vape cartridge market is dominated by one product: distillate. Walk into most dispensaries and the majority of the cartridge shelf — regardless of brand, regardless of price point — is THC distillate with terpenes blended back in.

That's not inherently a bad product. But it's a fundamentally different thing from a rosin vape, and the difference is worth understanding before you spend more money on a cart that claims to be premium.

## What's in a Standard Distillate Cartridge

Distillate is a highly refined cannabis oil produced through solvent extraction followed by distillation — a process that strips the extract down to near-pure THC (or CBD). The result is a clear, viscous oil with very high THC percentages, minimal terpenes, and minimal minor cannabinoids.

Because distillate is essentially flavorless and odorless, terpenes are added back in after processing to provide flavor and aroma. These terpenes may be cannabis-derived (CDT — cannabis-derived terpenes) or botanically-derived (BDT — terpenes extracted from non-cannabis plants like lavender, black pepper, or citrus fruits).

Distillate is also often mixed with a cutting agent to reduce viscosity for vaping hardware — MCT oil, PG, or other carriers. This became a major safety issue when the EVALI (e-cigarette associated lung injury) outbreak occurred in 2019, traced primarily to vitamin E acetate used as a cutting agent in unregulated cartridges. Reputable producers moved away from vitamin E acetate, but cutting agents in general remain common.

A standard distillate cart, then, contains: refined cannabis oil + added terpenes + (possibly) a cutting agent + whatever hardware it's in.

## What's in a Rosin Vape

A rosin vape contains live hash rosin — the same product that goes into a jar on the concentrate shelf — formulated for vaping hardware. No distillation, no solvent extraction, no added terpenes, no cutting agents.

Because live hash rosin is a whole-plant extract — containing the full spectrum of cannabinoids, terpenes, and minor compounds from the original fresh-frozen flower — it carries its own natural viscosity and flavor. The terpenes in a rosin vape are the terpenes from the plant, not something added afterward.

The hardware matters here too. Rosin requires specific vaping hardware designed for thicker, more viscous concentrates. The standard ceramic or wick hardware used in distillate carts burns rosin unevenly and at the wrong temperature. Quality rosin vapes use hardware with appropriate coil design and temperature calibration for full-spectrum concentrate.

## Why the Difference Matters

**Terpene authenticity.** Distillate-plus-added-terpenes tastes different from rosin with its natural terpene profile. Even cannabis-derived terpenes added back to distillate don't replicate the full expression of those compounds in their original context. The interaction between cannabinoids and terpenes in a whole-plant extract — sometimes called the entourage effect — isn't fully reproduced by recombining isolated components.

**No cutting agents.** Rosin is viscous enough to vape without dilution. There's nothing to add to thin it out, so cutting agents aren't part of the formula.

**Full cannabinoid spectrum.** Distillation removes minor cannabinoids in the process of concentrating THC. Rosin retains the minor cannabinoid profile of the original material — CBG, CBC, CBN, and others in the ratios the plant produced them.

**No residual solvents.** Rosin has no solvent residuals by definition. Distillate should be clean if properly processed, but residual solvents are a possible concern in poorly made product.

## The Trade-Offs

Rosin vapes cost more. The material is more expensive to produce than distillate, and the hardware requirements are more demanding. THC percentages are typically lower than distillate carts — rosin in the 65–80% THC range versus distillate commonly over 85–90%.

For some consumers, the raw THC number is the primary metric. Distillate delivers higher percentage numbers and a consistent, clean vapor. If you're looking for maximum potency per dollar in a vape format, rosin vapes are probably not the right purchase.

For consumers who value the full terpene expression of a specific cultivar, want to avoid cutting agents and residual solvents, and prefer a vape that behaves like the rosin on the concentrate shelf — rosin vapes are the direct answer.

## Kong's Approach to Vapes

Our rosin vapes use the same live hash rosin we produce for our concentrate lineup — sourced from our own fresh-frozen flower, washed into ice water hash, and pressed. The hardware is selected specifically for rosin viscosity and temperature requirements.

We don't use distillate, cutting agents, or added terpenes in our vape products. The flavor in the cart is the flavor of the cultivar. Nothing added, nothing removed.

If a vape claims to be "rosin" or "solventless" and doesn't come with a COA showing no residual solvents and a full-spectrum cannabinoid and terpene profile, ask more questions. The label doesn't guarantee the product.

---

*Kong Concentrates produces solventless rosin vapes alongside our full concentrate lineup in Grove, Oklahoma. All products are third-party lab tested. Available at licensed dispensaries across Oklahoma.*

posts.title: How Ice Water Hash Is Made: A Step-by-Step Breakdown
posts.description: Ice water hash — bubble hash — is the foundation of the solventless world. Here's exactly how it's made, what each step does, and where quality is won or lost.
keywords: how is ice water hash made
posts.og_title: How Ice Water Hash Is Made: A Step-by-Step Breakdown
posts.body:
Ice water hash — also called bubble hash, or simply water hash — is the foundation of the modern solventless movement. Almost every product labeled "solventless" either is ice water hash or was pressed from it. Understanding the process clarifies why quality varies so dramatically between producers.

Here's what actually happens from frozen flower to finished hash.

## Step 1: Fresh-Frozen Starting Material

The process begins at harvest. For live production, cannabis flower is harvested and immediately transferred to deep freeze — typically at -20°F or below — to lock in the trichome structure and terpene profile at peak freshness.

The quality ceiling of the finished hash is set at this step. Flower that wasn't grown for resin production, harvested at the wrong time, or handled poorly before freezing will not produce great hash regardless of how well the rest of the process goes. This is why single-source producers who grow their own material have a fundamental advantage: they control the starting point.

At Kong Concentrates, we grow the cultivars we wash specifically because it lets us manage the harvest-to-freeze window. The flower goes from plant to freezer on the same day.

## Step 2: Ice Bath Setup

The frozen biomass is submerged in ice water in a wash vessel — traditionally a large bucket or drum. The water temperature is kept as cold as possible throughout the wash, typically 34–38°F. Cold temperature keeps the trichome heads brittle, which allows them to break free cleanly from the stalk and plant material during agitation.

Trichomes at cold temperatures behave like frozen glass — they snap off at the connection point. Warm trichomes are flexible and tend to bend rather than break, resulting in more contamination in the final product. Keeping everything cold is one of the most important variables in the wash.

## Step 3: Agitation

The submerged biomass is agitated — either by hand, paddle, or machine mixer — to create mechanical action that breaks trichome heads free from the plant material. The trichomes sink to the bottom of the vessel as they separate (they're denser than water), while the plant material remains suspended or floats.

Agitation duration and intensity is a major variable. Aggressive agitation for a long time produces more yield but lower quality — more trichome stalks and plant material get broken up and end up in the collection. Gentler, shorter agitation is more selective and produces higher-grade material with less yield. Most experienced washers run multiple passes: a short, gentle wash to collect the highest-grade trichomes, followed by progressively more aggressive passes that produce higher yield at lower quality.

## Step 4: Filtering Through Micron Bags

The ice water slurry is then poured through a series of mesh bags at different micron ratings — typically ranging from 220 microns (coarsest, catches plant debris) down to 25 or 45 microns (finest, catches the smallest trichome heads).

Each bag collects a different size fraction of material. The "heads" fraction — the primary collection zone for full-melt quality material — typically falls in the 73–120 micron range, depending on the cultivar. The exact micron breakdown that produces the best results varies by plant and by batch.

The material in each bag is then collected onto a work surface for freeze-drying.

## Step 5: Freeze-Drying

Wet hash cannot be air-dried safely. Air-drying at room temperature, even with fans and climate control, invites mold development and allows terpene evaporation. The hash looks dry on the surface while remaining moist inside, and the aromatic compounds the whole process was designed to preserve begin to degrade.

Freeze-drying — lyophilization — solves this. The wet hash is spread on trays and placed in a freeze dryer, which uses a combination of freezing and vacuum pressure to remove moisture through sublimation: ice converts directly to vapor without passing through a liquid phase. This preserves the physical structure of the trichome heads and retains terpenes that would be lost in air-drying.

A quality freeze dryer is one of the most important equipment investments in a serious hash operation. The difference in final product between freeze-dried and air-dried hash is significant and immediately apparent in the aroma.

## Step 6: Grading and Quality Assessment

After freeze-drying, the hash from each micron fraction is assessed visually and by behavior — how it melts on a hot surface, how it smells, its color and texture. This assessment determines the grade (1–6 stars) and what the material becomes in its final form.

Highest-grade material (5–6 star) is allocated to full-melt offerings or premium rosin pressing. Mid-grade material is pressed into rosin at broader temperature ranges. Lower-grade material goes to edibles or other infused products where visual clarity and melt quality aren't the primary metric.

## Where Quality Is Won and Lost

Every step above contains variables that compound. Great starting material poorly washed produces mediocre hash. Mediocre starting material expertly washed produces the best possible mediocre hash. The ceiling is set by the plant; the floor is set by the process.

Producers who short any step — harvesting too late, washing too warm, air-drying instead of freeze-drying — can't recover those losses downstream. The final jar reflects every decision made from seed to press.

---

*Kong Concentrates produces single-source ice water hash and live hash rosin in Grove, Oklahoma. We grow our own flower, freeze at harvest, and process in-house from wash to press. Find us at licensed dispensaries across Oklahoma.*

posts.title: What Are Terpenes and Why Do They Matter in Cannabis Concentrates?
posts.description: Terpenes drive the flavor, aroma, and character of cannabis — and concentrates destroy them if made carelessly. Here's what terpenes are and how solventless extraction preserves them.
posts.og_title: What Are Terpenes and Why Do They Matter in Cannabis Concentrates?
posts.body:
Ask most dispensary customers what they're looking for in a concentrate and the answer is usually THC percentage. Ask the same question to most concentrate producers and the answer is terpenes.

That gap explains a lot about why some products that test at 85% THC feel flat while others at 72% feel alive. Terpenes are doing work that the cannabinoid panel doesn't capture.

## What Terpenes Are

Terpenes are volatile aromatic compounds produced by cannabis — and by essentially every other plant on earth. They're responsible for the smell of lavender, the bite of black pepper, the citrus in lemon peel, and the pine in a forest. Cannabis produces over 200 identified terpenes, though most individual cultivars express 10–20 in meaningful concentrations.

In cannabis, terpenes are produced in the same trichome glands that produce THC and other cannabinoids. They're not a separate layer of the plant — they're co-produced in the resin alongside the compounds that drive the cannabis effect.

Because terpenes are volatile — they evaporate at relatively low temperatures and oxidize when exposed to air and light — they're among the most fragile components of the cannabis plant. Every step of processing, from harvest through extraction and storage, is an opportunity to lose them.

## The Most Common Cannabis Terpenes

A few terpenes appear across a wide range of cannabis cultivars:

**Myrcene** — earthy, musky, slightly fruity. The most abundant terpene in many indica-leaning strains. Also present in hops and mangoes.

**Limonene** — bright citrus, lemon and orange notes. Common in sativa-leaning cultivars and strains with "lemon" or "orange" in their names.

**Caryophyllene** — spicy, peppery, woody. Unique among terpenes because it also binds to CB2 receptors in the endocannabinoid system, giving it physiological activity beyond aroma.

**Linalool** — floral, lavender-forward. Present in lavender itself; associated with calming effects.

**Terpinolene** — complex and variable: floral, herbal, and slightly piney. Common in strains like Jack Herer and Dutch Treat.

**Ocimene** — sweet, herbal, slightly citrusy. Common in tropical and haze-lineage cultivars.

**Pinene** — sharp pine and fresh air. Alpha-pinene and beta-pinene are both common in cannabis.

**Humulene** — earthy, woody, hoppy. Related to caryophyllene and also present in hops.

The specific combination and ratio of terpenes in a cultivar is called its **terpene profile** or "terp profile," and it's primarily responsible for the differences in smell and taste between strains.

## The Entourage Effect

The term "entourage effect" — introduced by researchers Raphael Mechoulam and Shimon Ben-Shabat in 1998 — describes the hypothesis that cannabinoids and terpenes interact synergistically, producing effects together that differ from the effects of isolated compounds alone.

The research on this is ongoing and contested in its specifics, but the practical observation — that whole-plant extracts produce a different experience than isolated THC at equivalent doses — is widely reported by both consumers and clinicians. Whether the mechanism is direct synergy, the physiological activity of specific terpenes like caryophyllene, or simple interaction effects is still being studied.

For concentrate consumers, the practical implication is straightforward: a full-spectrum extract that preserves the original terpene profile likely produces a different experience than a terpene-stripped distillate at comparable THC levels. That difference matters to some consumers and not to others.

## Why Concentrates Destroy Terpenes (and How Good Ones Don't)

Most concentrate production methods involve heat, solvents, or both — all of which degrade terpenes.

**Distillation** operates at temperatures that volatilize most terpenes along with the lower-boiling cannabinoids. A distillate COA showing minimal terpene content is accurate — distillation is designed to isolate specific compounds, which means everything else is removed. This is why terpenes are added back to distillate after the fact.

**BHO extraction** at improper temperatures or with aggressive purging can volatilize terpenes. Well-processed live resin BHO, made from fresh-frozen flower, can preserve significant terpene content — but the solvent process itself introduces thermal and chemical stress that solventless avoids.

**Ice water extraction (bubble hash)** is conducted entirely at cold temperatures with no solvents. The trichomes are separated mechanically from the plant material and freeze-dried. Cold temperature plus no chemical exposure is the ideal environment for terpene preservation.

**Rosin pressing** applies gentle heat and pressure. The temperature required to press hash into rosin is a tradeoff — too cold and the rosin won't flow, too hot and terpenes volatilize. Experienced rosin producers press at the lowest effective temperature for each cultivar, treating terpene retention as a primary quality metric.

The result of this care is a live hash rosin with a terpene profile that closely reflects the original fresh-frozen plant. On a COA, a well-made live rosin will show total terpene content in the 3–8% range (sometimes higher for particularly terpy cultivars), with a specific profile that matches the cultivar's expected expression.

## What to Look for on a COA

If a producer provides terpene testing on their COA — not all do — you can see exactly what's in the product:

- **Total terpene percentage** — a general indicator of how terp-rich the product is
- **Individual terpene breakdown** — what the specific compounds are and at what concentrations
- **Match to strain expectations** — if a product is labeled as a specific cultivar, the terpene profile should reflect what that cultivar is known to express

For rosin and ice water hash, the terpene COA is the best available proof that the extraction process preserved what the plant produced. It's the difference between a claim and a measurement.

---

*Kong Concentrates preserves the full terpene profile of our cultivars through cold-temperature ice water extraction and controlled rosin pressing. All products are third-party lab tested in Grove, Oklahoma. Available at licensed dispensaries across the state.*

posts.title: Growing Cannabis for Hash: What Makes a Cultivar Hash-Worthy
posts.description: Not every cannabis cultivar produces great hash. Here's what separates a hash-worthy plant from a flower-first cultivar — and why single-source production makes the difference.
posts.og_title: Growing Cannabis for Hash: What Makes a Cultivar Hash-Worthy
posts.body:
The solventless world has a saying: the best hash is made in the garden, not the lab.

Everything downstream — the wash, the press, the jar — is constrained by what the plant produced. You can optimize the process, but you can't exceed the raw material. Understanding what makes a cultivar hash-worthy is the foundation of understanding why the best solventless producers are, first and foremost, growers.

## The Primary Metrics: Trichome Size, Density, and Type

Not all cannabis trichomes are equal. The cannabis plant produces several types:

- **Capitate-stalked trichomes** — the large, mushroom-shaped glands visible to the naked eye on premium flower. These contain the highest concentration of cannabinoids and terpenes and are the primary target of ice water extraction.
- **Capitate-sessile trichomes** — smaller, with less developed stalks. Lower resin content than capitate-stalked.
- **Bulbous trichomes** — the smallest type, minimal resin content.

For hash production, the metric that matters most is the density and size of capitate-stalked trichomes. A cultivar that coats its flowers in large-headed, dense trichome coverage produces more resin to collect — and more of that resin will be in the size range that ice water bags are designed to capture.

Trichome **head size** matters because the wash bag micron rating is calibrated to catch heads within a specific diameter range. Small-headed trichomes on an otherwise frosty plant may fall through the collection bags at the micron sizes you're targeting. Large heads catch cleanly and separate more completely from the stalk — contributing to higher full-melt quality.

Trichome **stalk length** affects separation. Long-stalked trichomes are more likely to have the head separate cleanly from the stalk during agitation; short-stalked trichomes may come through with more stalk material attached, reducing quality.

## Secondary Metrics: Terpene Richness and Wash Behavior

A cultivar can look like a hash producer on paper — dense, large-headed trichomes — and still underperform in the wash. Some plants produce sticky resins that cling to plant material and don't separate cleanly regardless of water temperature or agitation. Others produce dry, granular resin that separates almost effortlessly.

Terpene richness is the other major factor. A cultivar with modest trichome density but exceptional terpene expression can produce rosin that punches well above its yield weight. The goal isn't maximum resin — it's maximum quality resin. Those are different targets.

The only definitive way to know how a cultivar performs is to wash it. Most experienced hash producers maintain records for every cultivar across every run — yield percentages by micron fraction, quality grades, terpene profiles — to understand exactly what each plant delivers under their specific conditions.

## Indoor vs. Outdoor vs. Greenhouse

Environment shapes trichome development. Cannabis plants produce trichomes as a stress response — protection against UV radiation, insects, and desiccation. Controlled indoor environments that modulate environmental stress in calculated ways can produce remarkably dense trichome coverage, but the tradeoff is that indoor flower produces less terpene diversity than plants that experience the natural stress variation of outdoor sun.

Outdoor and sun-grown cannabis often produces excellent terpene expression and high resin content, but exposure to weather, pests, and contamination introduces risk to the fresh-frozen product — especially microbial contamination that becomes a problem in the finished hash.

Most top-tier solventless producers use indoor or controlled-greenhouse cultivation specifically because the consistency and cleanliness of the starting material is essential. A single batch of mold-contaminated fresh-frozen material is a significant loss and potentially a microbial test failure on the finished product.

## How Kong Approaches Cultivar Selection

Kong Concentrates built our cultivation facility specifically because the fresh-frozen material we could source externally didn't consistently meet the standard we needed. We grow our own cultivars — selected for their hash production properties — and fresh-freeze at harvest under our direct control.

Our current lineup includes cultivars chosen for trichome density, head size, terpene richness, and wash behavior: Bacio Mints, Devil Driver, Grape Pie, Sticky Buns, and others. Each has been run enough times that we understand how it performs at our specific wash parameters — which bags it collects in, what its trichome size distribution looks like, how it expresses in the press.

The single-source model — grow, freeze, wash, press, all in-house — isn't just about marketing. It's about controlling every variable that affects the final product. When something underperforms, we can trace it back to a specific decision in the garden or the wash room, learn from it, and adjust.

That feedback loop is what makes the difference between producers who are improving every batch and producers who are at the ceiling of their source material.

## The Bottom Line for Consumers

When you see a concentrate labeled with a specific cultivar and a COA that shows a rich terpene profile, you're looking at the result of good growing choices as much as good extraction choices. The cultivar selection, growing environment, harvest timing, and fresh-freeze protocol all contribute to what's in the jar.

Ask your dispensary where the fresh-frozen material came from. Single-source producers who grow their own can answer that question with specifics. Others can't.

---

*Kong Concentrates grows, harvests, fresh-freezes, washes, and presses our own material in Grove, Oklahoma. All products are third-party lab tested. Find us at licensed dispensaries across Oklahoma.*

posts.title: How to Dab: A Beginner's Guide to Consuming Cannabis Concentrates
posts.description: Dabbing looks complicated from the outside. Here's a clear, practical guide to consuming cannabis concentrates — equipment, temperatures, and what to expect.
posts.og_title: How to Dab: A Beginner's Guide to Consuming Cannabis Concentrates
posts.body:
Dabbing has a reputation for being complicated. Torches, bangers, carb caps, temperature timers — the setup looks more like a chemistry lab than a consumption method. It's actually straightforward once you understand what each piece does and why temperature is the only variable that really matters.

This guide covers the basics: what you need, how to set it up, and what makes the difference between a great dab and a wasted one.

## The Equipment

**The Rig**

A dab rig is a water pipe designed for concentrates. It functions like a bong — water cools and filters the vapor — but with a joint size and fitting designed to accept a banger rather than a bowl. Rigs come in a wide range of sizes; smaller rigs (6–10 inches) are preferred by most concentrate consumers because they minimize the distance vapor travels, preserving terpene content before it reaches your lungs.

**The Banger**

The banger (also called a nail) is the heating element — the bucket-shaped piece that attaches to the rig where the concentrate is vaporized. Quartz is the most common and generally preferred material: it heats evenly, holds temperature well, and doesn't impart flavor. Ceramic bangers are also popular. Titanium heats fast but can affect flavor at higher temps.

Banger size and thickness affect heat retention. A thick-walled quartz banger holds temperature longer than a thin-walled one, giving you more time to work with the concentrate after heating.

**The Torch**

A butane torch is the standard heat source for concentrate consumption. Propane torches burn hotter and can overheat quartz faster; butane is more controllable. Use a torch sized appropriately for your banger — a large banger needs more sustained flame to reach temperature.

Electronic nails (e-nails) and electronic rigs (e-rigs) skip the torch entirely, maintaining a set temperature electronically. They cost more upfront but offer precision and consistency that torches can't match.

**The Carb Cap**

A carb cap is a cover placed over the banger after the concentrate is loaded. It restricts airflow, lowering the pressure inside the banger and allowing the concentrate to vaporize at lower temperatures than it otherwise could. Using a carb cap is not optional for low-temp dabbing — without it, you need higher temperatures to fully vaporize the concentrate, which burns off terpenes.

**The Dab Tool**

A metal, glass, or ceramic tool used to pick up concentrate and transfer it to the banger. Silicone tips are useful for sticky products like fresh-press rosin. Keep it clean.

## Temperature: The Only Variable That Really Matters

Dab temperature determines flavor, vapor volume, and efficiency. There is a meaningful spectrum:

**High temp (above 600°F)** — produces large, immediate vapor clouds. Also burns off most terpenes instantly and leaves residue ("chaz") in the banger. The concentrate vaporizes completely but the experience is harsh and flat-tasting.

**Mid temp (450–550°F)** — the range most consumers settle into. Good vapor production, reasonable terpene preservation, manageable harshness. A workable middle ground.

**Low temp (350–450°F)** — produces smaller, thinner vapor that is significantly more flavorful. Terpenes vaporize before cannabinoids, so the first part of a low-temp dab carries the full aromatic profile of the concentrate. Less vapor doesn't mean less effect — cannabinoid absorption is efficient at these temperatures. Some material may remain in the banger, which can be cleaned between dabs.

For live hash rosin — where you've paid for the terpene profile of a specific cultivar — low-temp dabbing is the only approach that makes sense. Torching the banger until it glows and loading your premium rosin is the equivalent of burning a good steak to char. The flavor is the point. Don't incinerate it.

## The Process, Step by Step

1. **Heat the banger** with your torch until the bottom and walls are uniformly hot. A full heat-up for a quartz banger typically takes 20–40 seconds depending on thickness and torch size.
2. **Let it cool.** This is the step most beginners skip. For low-temp dabbing, let the banger cool for 30–60 seconds after removing the torch flame. An infrared thermometer makes this precise; without one, you learn the timing for your specific banger through trial and error.
3. **Load the concentrate** using your dab tool. Touch the concentrate to the banger wall and it should immediately begin to melt and vaporize.
4. **Place the carb cap** and inhale slowly. Rotate directional carb caps to create airflow that moves pooled concentrate across the banger surface.
5. **Exhale and assess.** A well-executed low-temp dab produces milky, terpene-rich vapor. A too-hot dab produces harsh, acrid smoke. Adjust your cool-down time on the next dab accordingly.
6. **Clean the banger** with a cotton swab while it's still warm. This prevents buildup that degrades flavor over time.

## A Note on Starting Material

None of this technique matters if the concentrate isn't worth consuming carefully. The whole reason to dial in temperature and use a carb cap is to extract the maximum experience from the product — which means the product needs to have something worth extracting.

Live hash rosin from quality starting material, handled correctly through the extraction process, has a terpene profile worth preserving. Distillate at 90% THC doesn't have much terpene content to preserve; temperature matters less because there's less to lose.

Buy something worth tasting. Then taste it properly.

---

*Kong Concentrates produces live hash rosin, full-melt hash, and rosin vapes in Grove, Oklahoma. All products are single-source and third-party lab tested. Find us at licensed dispensaries across Oklahoma.*

posts.title: What Is a Rosin Press and How Does It Work?
posts.description: A rosin press uses heat and pressure to extract cannabis oil without solvents. Here's how the equipment works, what differentiates commercial from home presses, and what affects yield and quality.
posts.og_title: What Is a Rosin Press and How Does It Work?
posts.body:
The concept behind a rosin press is simple enough to explain in one sentence: apply heat and pressure to cannabis, and resinous oil flows out. The execution — doing it in a way that produces consistently high-quality results — is more involved.

Here's how the equipment works, what separates entry-level presses from commercial ones, and which variables actually matter for the final product.

## The Basic Mechanism

A rosin press consists of two heated plates that compress a cannabis source material — flower, kief, or hash — between them. The combination of heat and pressure causes the trichome cell walls to rupture and the resinous contents to flow out of the material. That flowing oil is collected on parchment paper or a collection vessel.

The process takes anywhere from a few seconds to a few minutes depending on the material type, plate temperature, and target outcome. The oil that flows is rosin — a full-spectrum, solventless concentrate containing cannabinoids, terpenes, and other plant compounds in ratios determined by the source material and pressing conditions.

That's the entire process. No solvents, no post-processing purge, no chemical inputs. Heat, pressure, parchment paper.

## Plate Materials and Design

Rosin press plates are most commonly made from stainless steel or aluminum with embedded heating elements that maintain a set temperature with minimal variance. Temperature uniformity across the plate surface matters — hot spots cause uneven flow and can scorch material at the edges while under-processing material in cooler zones.

Plate size determines how much material can be pressed in a single run. Small personal presses have plates measured in square inches; commercial presses have plates measured in square feet. Larger plates require proportionally more tonnage to achieve the same pounds-per-square-inch (PSI) across the pressing surface.

Plate gap matters for controlling pressure distribution. The gap between plates affects how the material deforms under pressure and how the oil channels escape the material.

## Pressure: PSI, Not Just Tons

Press manufacturers often advertise their equipment by tonnage — a 20-ton press, a 40-ton press. Tonnage alone is misleading without knowing the plate size.

The metric that matters for rosin quality is **pounds per square inch (PSI)** — how much pressure is applied per unit of plate surface area. A 20-ton press with small plates can produce very high PSI; the same 20-ton press with large plates produces lower PSI. Most hash rosin benefits from 300–1,000 PSI depending on consistency and material type.

Hydraulic presses provide more consistent, controllable pressure than pneumatic or hand-pump presses. At commercial scale, hydraulic systems allow precise pressure profiles — starting lower to allow the material to warm before applying full compression, which helps preserve terpenes and avoids blowouts.

## Temperature and Its Tradeoffs

Temperature is the primary variable that determines rosin consistency (texture) and terpene preservation.

**Lower temperatures (160–190°F)** produce rosin that retains more terpenes and typically presents as a saucier, more viscous consistency. Yield is lower because not all resin flows at lower temperatures. This is the preferred range for hash rosin from high-quality starting material.

**Higher temperatures (190–220°F)** produce more yield — more resin flows at higher heat — but at the cost of terpene loss and a drier, more stable consistency (badder, crumble). Color also darkens with higher temperatures.

The experienced presser finds the lowest temperature at which a given material produces acceptable yield. That temperature varies by cultivar and by the specific quality of the hash being pressed. Some materials need warmth to flow well; others flow easily at very low temperatures and are best pressed cold.

## Filter Bags

When pressing hash into rosin, the hash is typically enclosed in a mesh filter bag before pressing. The bag allows rosin to flow through while containing the plant material and trichome stalks that would contaminate the final product.

Filter bag micron rating determines what passes through. Finer microns (25–90µ) produce cleaner, lighter-colored rosin with less plant contamination; coarser microns (90–160µ) allow more material through, increasing yield at the cost of clarity. Most hash rosin producers use bags in the 25–73µ range.

Filter bags are not used when pressing flower directly — the flower itself acts as a filter, and the rosin flows out from the edges of the compressed material.

## Commercial vs. Home Pressing

Home rosin presses — from hair straightener setups to purpose-built personal presses — are capable of producing real rosin from quality starting material. The limitations are yield per run (limited plate size), temperature consistency (cheaper heating elements drift), and pressure control (most entry-level presses use manual or pneumatic systems that are harder to calibrate precisely).

Commercial presses in professional operations use larger hydraulic systems, PID temperature controllers that maintain precise setpoints, and plate designs optimized for specific material types. The difference isn't just scale — it's consistency across hundreds of consecutive runs.

At Kong Concentrates, our pressing operation is calibrated to the specific cultivars we grow and the specific hash quality we produce. We track yield, color, and terpene expression across runs and adjust pressing parameters when starting material changes. The press is a tool; the knowledge of how to use it for a given batch is what produces consistent results.

---

*Kong Concentrates presses live hash rosin from single-source fresh-frozen flower in Grove, Oklahoma. All products are third-party lab tested. Find us at licensed dispensaries across Oklahoma.*

posts.title: How to Store Cannabis Concentrates to Preserve Quality
posts.description: Cannabis concentrates degrade when exposed to heat, light, air, and moisture. Here's how to store rosin, hash, and other concentrates to preserve terpenes and potency.
posts.og_title: How to Store Cannabis Concentrates to Preserve Quality
posts.body:
You can buy the best rosin on the market and degrade it to mediocrity inside of two weeks with improper storage. The enemies are the same as for any perishable product: heat, light, oxygen, and moisture. Eliminate them and your concentrate stays close to what it was the day it was pressed. Ignore them and you're paying premium prices for a product that won't deliver.

Here's what you need to know.

## The Four Enemies of Concentrate Quality

**Heat** is the primary enemy. Terpenes are volatile — they evaporate at low temperatures under the right conditions, and heat accelerates this process dramatically. A rosin jar left on a warm surface or stored in a hot car loses terpene content rapidly. Even consistent room-temperature storage (above 70°F) causes measurable degradation over time. Cannabinoids also oxidize faster at elevated temperatures.
